28.       - Suggested restricting globs or using DefaultItemExcludes
29.       - Identified property function performing file I/O during evaluation phase
30.       - Explained difference between MSBuild evaluation phase and execution phase
31.

data.content:
Here are **three performance anti-patterns** in this project, all impacting the **MSBuild evaluation phase** (the phase where MSBuild parses the project file, resolves imports, expands properties, and collects items—*before* any targets execute):

---

### 1. Deep Import Chain (`level1` → `level2` → `level3`)

`EvalHeavy.csproj` imports `level1.props`, which imports `level2.props`, which imports `level3.props`. Each nested `<Import>` forces MSBuild to locate, open, parse, and evaluate another file **during evaluation**. Three levels may seem small, but in real projects these chains grow quickly and multiply evaluation time. **Fix:** flatten into a single `.props` file or reduce nesting depth.

### 2. Overly Broad Glob Pattern

```xml
<AdditionalFiles Include="**\*.*" Exclude="**\bin\**;**\obj\**" />
```

The glob `**\*.*` walks the **entire directory tree** during evaluation. It will scan into `.git/`, `node_modules/`, `packages/`, and any other large subdirectory—only excluding `bin/` and `obj/`. This can add seconds to evaluation in large repos. **Fix:** scope the glob to specific extensions or directories (e.g., `**\*.cs`) and leverage `$(DefaultItemExcludes)` which already excludes common noise directories.

### 3. File I/O via Property Function During Evaluation

```xml
<BuildNotes Condition="Exists('build-notes.txt')">
  $([System.IO.File]::ReadAllText('build-notes.txt'))
</BuildNotes>
```

This calls `System.IO.File.ReadAllText` as an **intrinsic property function**, which performs synchronous disk I/O during evaluation. Evaluation is supposed to be fast metadata processing; pulling file contents into a property forces blocking I/O and can be especially costly on network drives or CI agents. **Fix:** move file reading into a target (execution phase) using `ReadLinesFromFile` or a custom task, so it only runs when actually needed.

---

### Evaluation vs. Execution Phase

MSBuild processes a project in two phases. **Evaluation** parses XML, resolves imports, expands properties/items, and builds the in-memory project model—it should be purely declarative and fast. **Execution** runs targets and tasks. All three issues above inject expensive work (disk walking, file I/O, deep parsing) into evaluation, slowing every `dotnet build`, `dotnet restore`, and IDE project load.
